motion: This house would require all published AI research to include reproducibility artifacts.

conversation.speaker: moderator
conversation.text: Motion: all published AI research must include reproducibility artifacts. Pro opens.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: Irreproducible results are unfalsifiable claims. A 2020 survey found only 15% of accepted ML papers shipped runnable code, and independent replications of headline results routinely fail. Requiring artifacts converts marketing into science.

conversation.speaker: con
conversation.text: The mandate is regressive. Industrial labs with proprietary data simply won't publish — we lose the result entirely rather than gaining an artifact. Academic labs without engineering staff bear compliance costs that favor the already-resourced.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: The proprietary-data objection is handled by tiered artifacts: model cards, evaluation harnesses, and synthetic-data replications qualify. NeurIPS's checklist already operates this way and industrial submissions increased after its adoption.

conversation.speaker: con
conversation.text: If 'artifact' is diluted to a checklist and a model card, the motion no longer guarantees reproducibility — Pro has conceded the strong claim while keeping the strong rhetoric. A checklist mandate is the status quo, not this motion.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: I concede tiered artifacts weaken the guarantee, but they shift the default: today artifacts are optional everywhere; under the motion, absence requires justification. Defaults govern behavior — opt-out beats opt-in in every compliance domain.

conversation.speaker: moderator
conversation.text: Time. Both sides close: Pro on defaults and falsifiability, Con on exclusion effects and the diluted-mandate objection.

motion: This house believes coding agents should be allowed to merge changes without human review below a risk threshold.

conversation.speaker: moderator
conversation.text: Motion: coding agents may auto-merge low-risk changes. Pro opens.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: Human review of trivial changes is theater. Dependency bumps, formatting, and test-only changes have near-zero incident rates but consume 30% of review bandwidth. Gate on measured risk — blast radius, test coverage, rollback cost — not on ceremony.

conversation.speaker: con
conversation.text: Risk scoring is exactly what agents get wrong. The 2024 supply-chain incidents were 'low-risk dependency bumps.' Human review isn't about catching the average case — it's the last defense on the tail case the scorer mislabels.

conversation.speaker: pro
conversation.text: Tail risk argues for better gates, not human gates: reviewers rubber-stamp dependency bumps precisely because they can't audit them. Automated provenance checks catch what bored humans don't. The empirical question is which gate has the lower miss rate.

conversation.speaker: con
conversation.text: I accept that human review of lockfiles is weak. But the motion says 'without human review,' not 'with stronger automated review.' Accountability matters: when the auto-merged change ships an incident, who is responsible? Removing the human removes the accountable party.

conversation.speaker: moderator
conversation.text: Closing statements received. The bench will rule on whether accountability or empirical miss-rates carry the motion.
